concepts.title: Specific Conductance
concepts.conceptBody:
4. Conductance, Specific Conductance, Equivalent Conductance and Molar Conductance

The reciprocal of resistance offered by an electrolyte to the flow of electricity through it is known as conductance. The unit of conductance is $\text{oh}{{\text{m}}^{-1}}$ or mho.

The Ohm’s law relates the resistance ‘$\text{R}$’ offered by a medium with the applied voltage ‘$\text{V}$’ and the passing current ‘$\text{I}$’.

$\text{V}=\text{IR}$

The resistance ‘$R$’ is related to the dimensions of the conductors as:

$\text{R}\propto 1$

and $\text{R }\propto \frac{1}{\text{a}}$

$\therefore $ $\text{R}\propto \frac{\text{l}}{\text{a}};\text{R}=\rho \frac{\text{l}}{\text{a}}$

where $R=$ resistance, $\rho =$ specific resistance

or $\frac{1}{\rho }=\frac{1}{R}\times \frac{1}{a}$

$\therefore $ Specific conductance = Conductance $\times \frac{1}{\text{a}}$

Just as $\frac{1}{R}$ is called conductance, $\frac{1}{\rho }$ is called specific conductance denoted by ‘ $\text{K}$ ‘.

The solution under study is filled in a conductivity cell made of pyrex glass having two platinum electrodes fixed parallel to each other.

For a given cell, $\frac{\text{l}}{\text{a}}$ is known as cell constant. Thus:

Specific conductance = Conductance Cell constant 

Note: The unit of specific conductance $(\text{K})$ is $\text{oh}{{\text{m}}^{-1}}~\text{c}{{\text{m}}^{-1}}$ or $\text{Sc}{{\text{m}}^{-1}}$.

concepts.title: Molar Conductance
concepts.conceptBody:
Molar Conductance $(\wedge )$ 

The molar conductance is defined as the conductance of all the ions produced by the ionisation of $1\text{gm}$ mole of an electrolyte when present in $\text{Vml}$ of solution. It is denoted by ${{\wedge }_{\text{m}}}$ Molar conductance $\left( {{\wedge }_{\text{m}}} \right)=\text{K}\times \text{V}$ where $\text{V}$ is the volume in $\text{ml}$ containing $1\text{gm}$ mole of the electrolyte. If $\text{C}$ is the concentration of the solution in $\text{g}$ mole per litre, then:

$\wedge =K\times \frac{1000}{c}$

Its units are $\text{oh}{{\text{m}}^{-1}}~\text{c}{{\text{m}}^{2}}~\text{mo}{{\text{l}}^{-1}}$ or $\text{Sc}{{\text{m}}^{2}}~\text{mo}{{\text{l}}^{-1}}$.

concepts.title: Equivalent Conductance
concepts.conceptBody:
Equivalent Conductance $\left( {{\wedge }_{\text{eq}\text{. }}} \right)$ 

One of the factors on which the conductance of an electrolytic solution depends is the concentration of the solution. In order to obtain comparable results for different electrolytes, it is necessary take equivalent conductance.

Equivalent conductance is defined as the conductance of all the ions produced by one gram equivalent of an electrolyte in a given solution. It is denoted by ${{\wedge }_{\text{eq }}}$.

At concentration $\text{C}$ (in gm-equivalent/L i.e., normality), equivalent conductance.

${{\wedge }_{\text{eq}}}=\frac{1000\text{ specific conductance }}{\text{c}[\text{ or Normality }]}\left( \text{oh}{{\text{m}}^{-1}}~\text{c}{{\text{m}}^{-1}} \right)$

$=\frac{1000\times K}{N}$

concepts.title: Equivalent Conductance at infinite Dilution
concepts.conceptBody:
Equivalent Conductance at infinite Dilution

Equivalent conductance increases with the increase in dilution but after a limit it becomes constant and does not further increase. The maximum value of equivalent conductance is known as equivalent conductance at $\infty $ dilution, denoted by ${{\wedge }_{\infty }}$. If this increase in equivalent conductance with dilution is only due to the increase in the degree of dissociation of the electrolyte, we can write,

$\text{ degree of dissociation }\alpha =\frac{{{\wedge }_{\mathfrak{c}}}}{{{\wedge }_{\infty }}}$

concepts.title: Strong Electrolytes
concepts.conceptBody:
Variation of Molar Conductivity with Concentration

${{\wedge }_{\text{m}}}$ of electrolytes increases with dilution.

The variation is different for strong and weak electrolytes.

Strong Electrolytes 

It is given according to the equation $\wedge _{\text{m}}^{\text{c}}=\wedge _{\text{m}}^{\infty }-\text{b}\sqrt{\text{c}}$ (Debye Huckel onsager equation) $\wedge _{\text{m}}^{\text{c}}$ and $\wedge _{\text{m}}^{\infty }$ are the molar conductance at a given concentration and at infinite dilution (respectively). $\text{b}$ is a constant depending on the viscosity of the solvent. The graph shows that ${{\wedge }_{\text{m}}}$ decreases as the concentration increases.

This is because at higher concentration there is greater inter ionic attraction which retards the motion of the ions as conductance falls $\wedge _{\text{m}}^{\text{c}}$ and $\wedge _{\text{m}}^{\infty }$ is that conductance at infinite dilution where the ions are far apart and there is no inter-ionic attraction. This can be obtained by extrapolation of the graph to zero concentration.

concepts.title: Weak Electrolytes
concepts.conceptBody:
Weak Electrolytes 

A weak electrolyte dissociates to a much lesser extent so its conductance is lower than that of a strong electrolyte at the same concentration.

The very large increase at infinite dilution is because the ionization increases and so the number of ions in solution increases.

The value of $\wedge _{\text{m}}^{\text{c}}$ and $\wedge _{\text{m}}^{\infty }$ cannot be obtained by extrapolation as can be seen on the graph. It is obtained by applying Kohlrausch’s law.

${{\wedge }_{\text{m}}}$ values for strong electrolytes is larger than weak electrolytes for the same concentration.

Increase ${{\wedge }_{\text{m}}}$ for strong electrolyte is quite small as compared to that for weak electrolyte.

concepts.title: Relationship Between Molar and Equivalent Conductivities
concepts.conceptBody:
Relationship Between Molar and Equivalent Conductivities

According to the definition,

${{\wedge }_{\text{m}}}=\frac{\text{k}}{{{\text{C}}_{\text{m}}}}$ … (i)

and ${{\wedge }_{eq}}=\frac{\text{k}}{{{\text{C}}_{\text{eq}}}}$ … (ii)

For a solution containing a certain mass of solute per unit volume of the solution (let us say 
‘$\text{w}$’ gram per litre), we can write

${{\text{C}}_{\text{m}}}=\frac{\text{w}}{\text{ Molar mass of the electrolyte }}\text{ and }{{\text{C}}_{\text{eq}}}=\frac{\text{w}}{\text{ Equivalent mass of the electrolyte }}$

From these relations, we can write $\frac{{{\text{C}}_{\text{m}}}}{{{\text{C}}_{\text{eq}}}}=\frac{\text{ Equivalent mass of the electrolyte }}{\text{ Molar mass of the electrolyte }}$

We know that molar mass of an electrolyte $=\text{z}\quad $ Equivalent mass of electrolyte where $\text{z}$ is the number of equivalents of electrolytic charge per mole of the electrolyte. For example, n-factor of electrolyte).

$\therefore $ $\frac{{{\text{C}}_{\text{m}}}}{{{\text{C}}_{\text{eq }}}}=\frac{1}{\text{z}}$ …(iii)

Using equation (i) and (ii), we get

$\therefore $ $\frac{{{\text{C}}_{\text{m}}}}{{{\text{C}}_{\text{eq}}}}=\frac{{{\wedge }_{\text{eq}}}}{{{\wedge }_{\text{m}}}}$ ...(iv)

Then from equation (iii) and (iv), we get

$\frac{{{\text{C}}_{\text{m}}}}{{{\text{C}}_{\text{eq}}}}=\frac{1}{\text{z}}\quad \text{ or }\quad {{\wedge }_{\text{m}}}=\text{z}\times {{\wedge }_{\text{eq}}}$

where $\text{z}$ can have values equal to $1,2,3,\ldots $

concepts.title: Kohlraush's law
concepts.conceptBody:
Kohlrausch's Law

At infinite dilution an ionic specie (cation or anion) contributes a fixed value, at a given temperature, towards equivalent conductance of the electrolyte irrespective of the other ionic species in combination with it. There contributions are known as equivalent ionic conductance at $\infty $ dilution defined by $\lambda _{\text{c}}^{0}$ and $\lambda _{\text{a}}^{0}$ for cation and anion respectively. Mathematically,

$\wedge _{\text{m}}^{\infty }\text{ for }{{\text{A}}_{\text{x}}}{{\text{B}}_{\text{y}}}=\text{x}\lambda _{{{\text{A}}^{+}}}^{\infty }+\text{y}\lambda _{{{\text{B}}^{-}}}^{\infty }$

where $+\text{x}\lambda _{{{\text{B}}^{-}}}^{\infty }$ and $\text{y}\lambda _{{{\text{B}}^{-}}}^{\infty }$ are the major conductance of ${{\text{A}}^{+}}$and ${{\text{B}}^{-}}$at infinite dilution $\text{x}$ and $\text{y}$ are the ions provided by one formula unit of the electrolyte.

For example:

(1) $\wedge _{\text{m}}^{\infty }\text{ for NaCl}=\lambda _{\text{N}{{\text{a}}^{+}}}^{\infty }+\lambda _{\text{C}{{\text{l}}^{-}}}^{\infty }$

(2) $\wedge _{\text{m}}^{\infty }\text{ for BaC}{{\text{l}}_{2}}=\lambda _{\text{B}{{\text{a}}^{2+}}}^{\infty }+2\lambda _{\text{C}{{\text{l}}^{-}}}^{\infty }$

(3) $\wedge _{\text{m}}^{\infty }\text{ for A}{{\text{l}}_{2}}{{\left( \text{S}{{\text{O}}_{4}} \right)}_{3}}=2\lambda {{_{\text{Al}}^{\infty }}^{3+}}+3\lambda _{\text{SO}_{4}^{2-}}^{\infty }$

concepts.title: Applications of Kohlrausch’s Law
concepts.conceptBody:
Applications of Kohlrausch’s Law

Sometimes, the molar conductivity values for the ions are not available. In such cases, following procedure is adopted.

(i) Select a series of strong electrolytes such that the sum/difference of molar conductivities of their ions gives the molar conductivities of the ions of weak electrolyte.

(ii) Measure $\wedge _{\text{m}}^{\infty }$ values of these salts (strong electrolytes) at various concentrations $\left( {{\text{C}}_{\text{m}}} \right)$ and plot ${{\wedge }_{\text{m}}}$ against $\sqrt{{{\text{C}}_{\text{m}}}}$ for each salt separately. Determine $\wedge _{\text{m}}^{\infty }$ for each salt (strong electrolyte) by extrapolation method.

(iii) Add and/or subtract the equations to get the $\wedge _{\text{m}}^{\infty }$ of the weak electrolyte. Let us determine the molar conductivity of a weak electrolyte, MA at infinite dilution. For this purpose, we take three salts $\text{MCl},\text{NaA}$ and $\text{NaCl}$ and determine their $\wedge _{\text{m}}^{\infty }$ values by extrapolation method.

Calculation of molar conductivity at infinite dilution (A) for weak electrolytes $\left( \text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{COOH} \right)$

$\wedge _{\left( \text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{COOH} \right)}^{\infty }=\lambda _{\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{CO}{{\text{O}}^{-}}}^{\infty }+\lambda _{{{\text{H}}^{+}}}^{\infty }$

The value of ${{\wedge }^{\infty }}$ for $\text{KCl},\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{COOK}$ and $\text{HCl}$ can be obtained by extrapolation (they are strong electrolytes).

${{\wedge }^{\infty }}(\text{KCl})=\lambda _{{{\text{K}}_{+}}}^{\infty }+\lambda _{\text{C}{{\text{l}}^{-}}}^{\infty }$

${{\wedge }^{\infty }}\left( \text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{COOK} \right)=\lambda _{\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{CO}{{\text{O}}^{-}}}^{\infty }+\lambda _{\text{K}+}^{\infty }$

${{\wedge }^{\infty }}(\text{HCl})=\lambda _{{{\text{H}}^{+}}}^{\infty }+\lambda _{\text{C}{{\text{l}}^{-}}}^{\infty }$

$\therefore $ $\wedge _{\text{CH}}^{\infty }\text{COOH}=\wedge _{\text{CH}}^{\infty }\text{COOK}+\wedge _{\text{HCl}}^{\infty }-\wedge _{\text{KCl}}^{\infty }$

concepts.title: Applications of conductance measurement
concepts.conceptBody:
Applications Of Conductance Measurement

  • Conductance is measured based on the “Wheatstone Bridge” principle.
  • Conductivity cell is calibrated using saturated, $1~\text{N}$ or $0.01~\text{NKCl}$ solution. Their specific conductances at a given temperature are constant values. If their conductances are known then,

$\text{cell constant }=\frac{\text{ specific conductance }}{\text{ conductance }}$

  • Generally all measurement are made using conductivity water. If conductivity water is not available then

True conductance$=[$exptl value - conductance of water]

  • Conductance of conductivity water is taken as zero.

concepts.title: Degree of Ionisation
concepts.conceptBody:
Degree of Ionisation and Ionisation Constant:

If ${{\wedge }_{c}}=$ equivalent conductance at concentration $\text{C}$ gm equivalent ${{\text{L}}^{-1}}$

${{\wedge }_{0}}=$ equivalent conductance at infinite dilution

then $\text{x}=\frac{{{\wedge }_{\text{c}}}}{{{\wedge }_{0}}}$

For weak acid, using Oswald’s dilution law

$\text{HA}\rightleftharpoons {{\text{H}}^{+}}+{{\text{A}}^{-}}$

$\left. {{\text{K}}_{\text{a}}}\text{ (ionisation constant of a weak acid } \right)=\frac{\text{C}{{\text{x}}^{2}}}{(1-\text{x})}$

Similarly ${{\text{K}}_{\text{b}}}$ (ionisation constant of a weak base) can be calculated.

concepts.title: Solubility Product of a sparingly Soluble Salt
concepts.conceptBody:
Solubility Product of a sparingly Soluble Salt:

If solute is sparingly soluble in a given solvent, its concentration is taken as its solubility in the saturated solution. Also Lt $\text{C}\to 0{{\wedge }_{\text{c}}}={{\wedge }_{0}}$

Thus ${{\wedge }_{\text{c}}}={{\wedge }_{0}}=\frac{1000\times \text{ sp}\text{. conductance }}{\text{C}}$

${{\wedge }_{0}}$ can be computed by use of Kohlrausch’s law

Thus $\text{C}$ (which is also the solubility of the sparingly soluble salt) and hence ${{\text{K}}_{\text{sp}}}$ is known.

Solute ${{\mathbf{K}}_{\text{sp}}}$ 

$\text{AgCl}$ ${{\text{K}}_{\text{sp}}}=\left[ \text{A}{{\text{g}}^{+}} \right]\left[ \text{C}{{\text{l}}^{-}} \right]={{\text{S}}^{2}}$

$\text{Pb}{{\text{I}}_{2}}$ ${{\text{K}}_{\text{sp}}}=\left[ \text{P}{{\text{b}}^{2+}} \right]{{\left[ {{\text{I}}^{-}} \right]}^{2}}=4~{{\text{S}}^{3}}$

$\text{Al}{{(\text{OH})}_{3}}$ ${{\text{K}}_{\text{sp}}}=\left[ \text{A}{{\text{l}}^{3+}} \right]{{\left[ \text{O}{{\text{H}}^{-}} \right]}^{3}}=27~{{\text{S}}^{4}}$

$~{{\text{A}}^{\text{x}}}{{\text{B}}^{\text{y}}}$ ${{\text{K}}_{\text{sp}}}={{\text{x}}^{\text{x}}}{{\text{y}}^{\text{y}}}{{(\text{S})}^{\text{x}+\text{y}}}$

S is the solubility in mol ${{\text{L}}^{-1}}$

concepts.title: Quantitative aspectss of electrolysis
concepts.conceptBody:
Qualitative Aspect Of Electrolysis

Decomposition of a compound into its constituents by passing electricity is called electrolysis. It consists of an electrolytic solution in a container. Two electrodes are dipped in it and they are connected to a battery. When the electricity is passed, the anion moves to the anode and gets oxidized. This releases electrons, these electrons pass through the outer circuit jump through the battery and these electrons are available at the cathode for reduction of cations.

At anode, $\quad \text{C}{{\text{l}}^{-}}\to \text{Cl}+1{{\text{e}}^{-}}$(oxidation)

At cathode, $\text{N}{{\text{a}}^{+}}+{{\text{e}}^{-}}\to \text{Na}$ (reduction)
